coredata.dc:title: One-step large-scale fabricating aggregation-induced emission carbon dots with strong solid-state fluorescence emission
coredata.dc:creator: Li, J.
Gong, X.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) possessing favorable stability and optical properties have attracted extensive attention for their potential application in solid-state light-emitting devices. However, due to the stacking of π–π conjugated systems, solid-state CDs often suffer aggregation-caused quenching effect, resulting in fluorescence quenching. There are few reports on solid-state fluorescent CDs fabricated without using other solid matrices. In this work, we report a facile approach for one-step synthesis of yellow-green solid-state CDs without using matrix. The synthesized CDs show an independent excitation and emission in various organic solvents. Importantly, the prepared CDs show solvatochromism and aggregation-induced emission redshift effect. By simply drying the colloidal solution of CDs, yellow-green solid-state fluorescent CD powders with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 25.5% can be obtained. High-performance white light-emitting diodes with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage coordinates at (0.35, 0.37) are prepared by employing the yellow-green solid-state CD powders. The corresponding color temperature and color rendering index are 4691 K and 91.5 under ultraviolet excitation of 365 nm, respectively. The facile synthesis method of solid-state fluorescent CDs with unique optical features offers promising future for CDs-based optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Carbon dots
Solid-state fluorescence
Matrix-free

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098 ), and the 111 project (No. B18038 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of synthesizing CDs and solid-state CDs. Photographs of solid-state CDs under daylight (b) and 365 nm UV light (c). (d) The typical TEM image of CDs. Inset is the corresponding HRTEM image. (e) Size distribution histogram of CDs obtained from TEM images. (f) XPS spectrum of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) C 1s, (h) N 1s, and (i) O 1s. CD, carbon dot; HRTEM, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy; TEM, transmission electron microscopy; UV, ultraviolet; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

Optical properties obtained both in solution (top row) and in the solid state (bottom row). (a) UV–vis absorption (blue line) and PL emission spectra (red line) of CDs in solution. (b) CIE color coordinates of CDs in solution. (c) PL emission spectra of CDs in solution at different excitation wavelengths. (d) UV–vis absorption (blue line) and PL emission spectra (red line) of CDs in the solid state. (e) CIE color coordinates of CDs in the solid state. (f) PL emission spectra of CDs in the solid state at different excitation wavelengths. CD, carbon dot; CIE, Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage; PL, photoluminescence; UV, ultraviolet.

(a) Normalized emission of CDs in different solvents. (b) Emission peak of spectra from (a). (c) Plot of emission maxima ET from (a) as a function of
E T N
solvent polarity parameter; the line is a linear fit of the data. (d) Normalized excitation spectra of CDs in different solvents corresponding to their emission wavelength. CD, carbon dot.

Photographs of the as-prepared CD in ethanol solution with varying volume ratios of water (from 0 to 100%) under sunlight (a) and 365 nm UV irradiation (b). (c) PL emission spectra of the CD as-prepared in ethanol solution with varying ratios of water. (d) Fluorescence intensity trend of the CDs in ethanol solution by varying the ratio of water. (e) Normalized PL of CDs in different ratios of water. CD, carbon dot; PL, photoluminescence; UV, ultraviolet.

Applications of solid-state CDs. (a) and (b) Optical images of the monochromatic LED constructed using CDs. (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of the monochromatic LED constructed using CDs. (d) PL emission spectra of the monochromatic LED constructed using CDs. (e) and (f) Optical images of the white LED constructed using CDs and commercial phosphors. (g) CIE chromaticity coordinates of the white LED constructed using CDs and commercial phosphors. (h) PL emission spectra of the white LED constructed using CDs and commercial phosphors. CD, carbon dot; CIE, Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage; LED, light-emitting diode;
The fluorescence lifetime of the yellow-green carbon dots in different states.

head.ce:title: One-step large-scale fabricating aggregation-induced emission carbon dots with strong solid-state fluorescence emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possessing favorable stability and optical properties have attracted extensive attention for their potential application in solid-state light-emitting devices. However, due to the stacking of π–π conjugated systems, solid-state CDs often suffer aggregation-caused quenching effect, resulting in fluorescence quenching. There are few reports on solid-state fluorescent CDs fabricated without using other solid matrices. In this work, we report a facile approach for one-step synthesis of yellow-green solid-state CDs without using matrix. The synthesized CDs show an independent excitation and emission in various organic solvents. Importantly, the prepared CDs show solvatochromism and aggregation-induced emission redshift effect. By simply drying the colloidal solution of CDs, yellow-green solid-state fluorescent CD powders with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 25.5% can be obtained. High-performance white light-emitting diodes with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage coordinates at (0.35, 0.37) are prepared by employing the yellow-green solid-state CD powders. The corresponding color temperature and color rendering index are 4691 K and 91.5 under ultraviolet excitation of 365 nm, respectively. The facile synthesis method of solid-state fluorescent CDs with unique optical features offers promising future for CDs-based optoelectronic devices.

A facile approach for large-scale one-step synthesis of yellow-green solid-state carbon dots without using matrix which show a photoluminescence quantum yield of 25.5% is reported. High-performance white light-emitting diodes with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage coordinates at (0.35, 0.37) are fabricated by employing the solid-state carbon dot powders. The corresponding color temperature and color rendering index are 4691 K and 91.5 under ultraviolet excitation of 365 nm, respectively.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of zero-dimensional luminescent material with a small size of less than 10 nm, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted a lot of interest due to a number of unique merits including outstanding optical properties [1–5], high stability [6–10], low cytotoxicity [11–13], good biocompatibility [13–16], environmental-friendliness [17,18], simple preparation [19–21], easy functionalization [22–24], and tunable excitation and emission spectra [19,25–31]. Moreover, extensive sources of raw materials and diverse synthetic methods for CDs are further in favor of their applications [32–34]. As a solid-state fluorescent material, CDs have been widely studied in the fields of lighting and displays because of their excellent properties [35]. However, owing to excessive resonant energy transfer or π–π direct interaction, most CDs exhibit aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in solid/powder state, which is one of the major issues limiting their application in the solid state [3,36–38]. Particularly, the ACQ effect of CDs greatly hinders their wide applications in solid-state optoelectronic devices or sensors relying on residual solid-state fluorescence [12,39,40]. Therefore, solid-state fluorescent CDs that can effectively avoid ACQ effect are crucial to realize their lighting application.

Usually, in the solid state or high concentration, the fluorescence of CDs will be seriously weakened or directly quenched by aggregation. To address this issue, a series of encapsulation methods to resist ACQ has been developed, which are to immobilize CDs in solid matrices such as polymers [41–51], hybrid organic-inorganic sol-gel materials [35,52–55], mesoporous materials [56–59], 2D layered materials [60,61], acid molecules [62,63], inorganic salts [64], and metal oxides [65–67]. Dispersing CDs into these solid matrices can not only retain the fluorescence properties like the original CD solution but also enhance the optical and thermal stability of CDs. This physical approach can increase the distance between CD particles, which makes the dispersion of CDs more uniform, so as to avoid the self-quenching of CDs in the solid state [35]. However, this approach requires mutual selectivity between CDs and the matrix, which cannot really eliminate the inherent ACQ effect of pure CDs in the solid. Especially, the matrices used may result in weakened fluorescence and staining capability. Therefore, realizing solid-state fluorescence properties of CDs without the ACQ effect and with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) is not only a difficult problem to be solved but also a hot spot in the research of solid-state CDs. A more effective strategy is to make CDs becoming special solid powder, and its emission fluorescence properties are not determined by ACQ, but by the opposite phenomenon, namely aggregation-induced emission (AIE), so as to obtain high PLQYs [46]. Although a few common methods can solve the ACQ problem of CDs to some extent, they are accompanied by complex multi-step preparation process, which are time-consuming and have some problems of mutual selectivity and instability. To date, there are few reports on directly preparing solid-state fluorescent CDs by one-step method, especially without matrix and via in-situ synthesis. Thus, it is still a huge challenge to develop a one-step approach for the direct in-situ synthesis of solid-state self-quenching-resistant CDs without using a matrix.

Here, we developed a facile yet efficient method for one-step direct synthesis of yellow-green emissive CDs with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) which can be used for light-emitting diode (LED) applications. The preparation process of the yellow-green solid-state CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Yellow-green emission CDs with AIEE character under the solid state were synthesized through a solvothermal method with resorcinol (Rc) and m-phenyldimethylamine in ethanol. CD solution and CD powders both show bright yellow-green emission. Most importantly, the solid CDs could maintain a strong fluorescence without the help of any matrix. Our strategy can effectively avoid the ACQ effect and realize the AIEE effect of CDs. The as-synthesized yellow-green CDs exhibited high PLQYs in the solid state. In addition, the prepared CDs showed almost independent characteristics of excitation wavelength, which can easily form solid-state fluorescent CDs by simply drying the colloidal CD solution, showing broad application prospects in solid-state lighting. PL intensity of CDs increased with the increase of CD solution concentration. The AIEE property of CDs was further confirmed by using ethanol as a good solvent and water as a poor solvent. Finally, the solid-state yellow-green CDs were successfully applied to the manufacture of high-performance white light-emitting diodes with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.35, 0.37) and a color rendering index (CRI) of 91.5 under ultraviolet (UV) excitation of 365 nm.

Yellow-green solid-state CDs were synthesized according to a solvothermal method. By optimizing the experimental conditions, the optimum reaction temperature and time were 180 °C and 6 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1a, the yellow-green solid-state CDs with AIEE were in-situ synthesized by one-step synthesis with ethanol as solvent. The prepared CD solution was then simply filtered and directly poured into the culture dish for natural volatilization. After the solvent volatilizes completely, the solid CDs were slightly and gently ground to obtain the solid CD powder so that the low-cost large-scale synthesis of solid CDs could be easily realized. The obtained CD powder in the natural state is shown in Fig. 1b. When illuminated with 365 nm UV light, the CD powder shows yellow-green luminescence (Fig. 1c). The morphology and structure of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The results reveal that the morphology and size of the CDs in ethanol are regular quasi-spherical particles (Fig. 1d). The CDs have a broad size distribution with an average particle size of 5.2 nm as illustrated in Fig. 1e. The HRTEM image shows that the CDs possess a good crystal structure. It is observed that the crystal plane spacing between lattice fringes is 0.21 nm, which is basically close to the diffraction facets of graphite carbon (100) [3,35]. The surface state and possible chemical groups of CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As illustrated in Fig. S1, the result confirms that the chemical composition of the yellow-green CDs is similar to the reported results [3,68,69].

In the FT-IR spectra, the absorption peak ranging from 3300 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1 results from the stretching vibrations of N–H or O–H. The absorption peak ranging from 2800 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1 indicates the existence of stretching vibrations of C–H. One peak at 1645 cm−1 attributed to stretching vibration of C=C or C=N is observed. Another peak at 1381 cm−1 is originated from stretching vibrations of C–C or C–N, and one absorption peak from 1028 cm−1 to 1199 cm−1 belongs to a bending vibration of C–H or C–O. Therefore, FT-IR results show that the core structure of the obtained yellow-green CDs contains conjugated bonds and primary amino groups of precursor molecules. In addition, X-ray diffraction and Raman were also used to characterize the structure of CDs, as shown in Fig. S2. The X-ray diffraction pattern displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 25.2° (Fig. S2a), which confirms the (002) lattice plane of graphite carbon [18,68]. As shown in Fig. S2b, the Raman spectrum of the CDs presented two distinct characteristic peaks, corresponding to the D peak at nearly 1350 cm−1 and the G peak at approximately 1580 cm−1, respectively [18]. In general, the intensity ratio (ID/IG) of characteristic Raman peaks can be used to study the structural properties of carbon skeletons, especially the crystallinity and relative abundance of carbon core and surface atoms [70,71]. It can be found that ID/IG = 0.96, which may be that the tiny graphitized CDs are embedded in some disordered carbon. The chemical composition and bonding form of the synthesized yellow-green CDs were further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. According to Fig. 1f, the full X-ray photoelectron spectrum shows three significant characteristic peaks of C, N, and O, corresponding to C 1s (284.4 eV), N 1s (399.4 eV), and O 1s (531.8 eV), respectively. The element content of C, N, and O is 72.21%, 14.81%, and 12.98%, respectively, suggesting that these elements are the main components of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1g, in the separate fitting peak spectra, the C 1s spectrum can be decomposed into four peaks of ∼284.6 eV, ∼285.8 eV, ∼288.8 eV, and ∼290.8 eV, which belong to C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O, and C=N groups, respectively. And also as illustrated in Fig. 1h, the N 1s spectrum could be decomposed into three peaks of ∼397.6 eV, ∼399.4 eV, and ∼403.2 eV, which belong to pyridine-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N groups, respectively. The O 1s spectrum of the CDs also could be decomposed into two oxygen peaks of C–O at 529.6 eV and O–H at 530.7 eV (Fig. 1i).

UV–visible absorption spectra of the yellow-green CDs show that the solution and solid samples have similar absorption phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 2 a, d, in the absorption spectrum of CD solution (Fig. 2a), the major absorption peak reaches the maximum at 270–300 nm, while the second strongest peak is concentrated at 347 nm, and the tail of absorption can extend to the visible region. The absorption peak at 270–300 nm is ascribed to the π–π∗ transition of C=C in the sp2 domain, and the peak at 300–500 nm may be attributed to the n–π∗ transition caused by the excited state energy of the surface state for the trapped by CDs, such as the related C=O and C=N bonds [3,35,68]. In addition, it is clearly seen that the absorption range in a solid state is significantly wider and stronger than that in solutions. Their emission spectra profiles are similar in solution and solid state, reaching the maximum near 540 nm, and the whole peak span varies from 400 nm to 700 nm. In detail, Fig. 2a shows that the excitation wavelength is 431 nm, and the PL peak reaches the maximum PL intensity at 536 nm. However, Fig. 2d shows the PL peak centers at 542 nm and the maximum PL intensity is obtained when the excitation wavelength is 457 nm. In addition, it is clear that the PL intensity of the solid-state CDs shows a slight redshift in the emission and the excitation. Since the Förster distance (R0) may cause spectral overlap between absorption and emission, when the distance between particles in the R0 range is very small, resonance energy transfer will occur, leading to redshift [35,48]. Therefore, the emission spectrum of solid-state fluorescence of CDs in the short wavelength region overlaps with the absorption between 400 and 550 nm and is weakened by energy transfer, which may be a possible factor resulting in the redshift of the aggregate state (solid state). To verify the resonance energy transfer process of the solid-state CDs, fluorescence decay tests were carried out in solid and solution states (Fig. S3). As shown in Table 1 , it is clearly detected that the average lifetime of CDs in the solid state (2.73 ns) is much shorter than that in the solution (4.54 ns). Since the obvious competition between resonance energy transfer and radiation transition can lead to the shortening of fluorescence lifetime, this result indirectly and strongly demonstrates the occurrence of resonance energy transfer (see the detailed lifetime-related calculations in Supplementary Information). The CIE color coordinates of the two samples were extracted from the PL emission spectra, showing the CIE coordinates of CDs in solution and solid state are (0.30, 0.62) and (0.32, 0.62), respectively (Fig. 2b, e). It is obvious that the PL emission between the two basically does not change, indicating that the curing transformation of CDs does not lead to the ACQ effect and still retains the fluorescence of the original solution. Synchronously, to better analyze the optical properties, the excitation-dependent characterization of CDs in different states (solution and solid state) was carried out (Fig. 2c, f). With the excitation wavelength increasing, the PL emission peaks of CDs in solution (Fig. 2c) show an obvious redshift. However, for solid-state CDs, it can be observed that the position of the PL emission peak center hardly changes with the change of excitation wavelength. It is worth noting that the excitation-dependence of CDs between the two states is different, which is attributed to the change of CD state from solution to solid. As we know, the dispersion of solid-state CDs is not as well as that in liquid. When the dispersion of CDs is relatively inhomogeneous, there may be more transition modes, and energy level splitting and broadening may occur during CDs aggregation, and the energy gap may also be reduced. In order to further study the difference in optical properties between solution and solid powder, the PL spectra of CDs in the solid state were compared with those in solutions with different concentrations. When the concentration of CD solution increases from 0.2 mg/mL to 80 mg/mL, the PL intensity increases significantly (Fig. S4). When the solid powder is obtained by drying CD solution, the PL intensity of the powder is about twice that of the original solution, which is consistent with the traditional AIEE [36]. In addition, the PL intensity of the CD powder remarkably declines as the temperature increases (Fig. S5). This can be explained by that the low temperature limits the movement of surface groups, thus enhancing the radiation decay. This is similar to the characteristics of typical temperature-sensitive fluorescent nanomaterials [72,73].

To investigate the interaction between CDs and different solvents, we dispersed the CDs in solvents with different solvation capabilities to reveal the interaction by solvent-dependent emission behavior of CDs. The emission spectra under different solvents show obvious solvent-dependent shifts (Fig. 3 a), and the peak position changes obviously in different solvents (Fig. S6). It can be clearly seen from the corresponding peak position in Fig. 3b that with the increase of solvent polarity, the maximum PL emission peak from toluene (2.638 eV) to water (2.255 eV) is red-shifted by about 0.383 eV, indicating that the solvent stabilizes the excited state [74–76]. Due to the photophysical process of CDs in a solvent, the solvent solvatochromism of CDs will induce the charge transfer process [76]. There have been a few reports on solvatochromic studies, and these studies showed that there is rather a significant shape change or no obvious change in PL emission peak [75]. Interestingly, in our case, there is an obvious redshift trend in the PL emission peak (Fig. 3a), but the PL emission peak shape does not change substantially, which is different from the previous studies. Importantly, it is clearly observed that the PL emission of CDs has a large redshift in water compared with that in organic solvents. These changes can be explained by that the PL behavior of these CDs is similar to that of solvent sensitive molecules used as polar indicators because most of them show solvatochromic absorption or emission shifts between weak polar and strong polar solvents [77]. In addition, the PL properties of CDs are quite different from those of traditional organic fluorophores, because the latter does not have tunable fluorescence effect. The solvatochromism of CDs can be effectively described by measuring the overall solvation ability of solvents as a combination of non-specific (such as dipole dipole) and specific (HB) interactions through the so-called
E T N
that is standardization parameter of the polarity scale [75,77]. As shown in Fig. 3c, it can be observed that there is no obvious linear correlation between the emission transition energy (ET) of CDs and the normalization parameter (
E T N
) of solvation ability [77]. But the change of CDs in aqueous solution is significantly different from that in other solvents, revealing that the emission of CDs in water is not only affected by polarity but also affected by other factors. In addition, the optimal excitation spectra of CDs were measured to further determine the effect of solvent polarity on ground state stability. In the photoluminescence excitation spectrum presented in Fig. 3d, only a slight shift of the photoluminescence excitation spectra appears in different polar solvents, revealing that the polarity of the solvent does not significantly interfere with the ground state of CDs, although abnormal behavior is found in the case of water [76]. Therefore, the results show that organic solvents have little effect on the ground-state stability of CDs. However, the aqueous medium has a huge effect on the ground state of CDs. The excitation spectrum of CDs in an aqueous medium is quite different from that of other solvents, and the peak pattern is wider, which proves that the Stokes shift observed in water is not caused by the solvatochromic effect of solvents.

As described above, the linear deviation relationship of ET value of CDs in water was examined by the plot of ET vs.
E T N
. Using the relatively poor solubility of CDs in an aqueous solution and large red-shifting emission characteristics, it is a good choice to check the properties of AIE. Ethanol and water were used as good and poor solvents, respectively, to further verify the AIEE effect of the CDs. The CD concentration was 0.16 mg/mL during the AIEE experiment. More intuitively, it can be seen from the photographs in Fig. 4 a that the prepared pure CD solution and the solution with water content less than 60% (volume ratio) are colorless to light brownish yellow-green homogeneous liquids. When the volume ratio of water is higher than 60%, the mixed solution (ethanol and water) of CDs begins to turn into a turbid milky liquid. Under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4b), the fluorescence of the above liquid was cyan to yellow-green, while the turbid liquid presented a tender yellow-green fluorescence. Therefore, the luminescence color of CDs also changes in different water proportion content. To better explain this phenomenon, as shown in Fig. 4c, at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the PL spectra of CDs increase with the increase of water content. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 4d, with the water content continues to increase, the PL emission intensity of CDs gradually decreases, and the spectral emission position has a slight redshift (Fig. 4e), which can be attributed to the aggregation behavior of CDs. Conversely, with the continuous increase of water content in ethanol, the PL emission (about 552 nm) intensity of CDs decreases abnormally. After adding more than 70% water into CD ethanol solution, the homogenously transparent state of CDs solution gradually transforms into a turbid solution state. This phenomenon indicates the formation of aggregation [3,18,76]. TEM images further verify the formation of aggregation (Fig. S7). It can be clearly seen that the degree of aggregation is also different with the different water content. When the volume ratio of ethanol to water is 10%, the aggregation formation of CDs can be clearly detected in the TEM images, that is, CDs are distributed in an aggregate material (Figs. S7a and b). However, when the water content increases up to 100%, the TEM image clearly shows that the aggregation degree of CDs is significantly higher than that when the water content is 10% (Figs. S7c and d). Significantly, the HRTEM image of CDs aggregates (Fig. S7d) shows that there are different carbon lattice planes in CDs aggregates, that is, the crystal phases corresponding to i, ii and iii spots are inconsistent. In other words, the assembly direction of CDs is random [18]. This reveals the relationship between the luminescence mechanism of CDs and their dispersion state. Also, it is able to be seen from the PL spectrum that with the addition of water, there is a peak position shift of nearly 50 nm between the spectra (Fig. 4e). Therefore, AIEE effect can well explain the behavior change of PL in CD solution and these results also demonstrate that there is the existence of complex aggregates in our CD system [18]. This phenomenon is consistent with the widely recognized and accepted AIE [3,78–80]. That is, the highly viscous medium causes restriction of rotation, and the excited state relaxation occurs through enhanced fluorescence emission [3,18]. The PLQYs of the CDs in the solid state, good solvent, and bad solvent can also indicate the enhanced emission of this AIE (Fig. S8).

Due to its unique properties and excellent performances, solid-state fluorescent CD powder can meet the requirements of many important applications. To verify the potential application of CD phosphor powders with yellow-green fluorescence as a solid-state luminescent material in lighting, CD powders were directly applied to LED as a phosphor. The fluorescent film containing CDs was covered on the LED UV chip (365 nm) to construct the LED. The photographs in Fig. 5 a and b show the monochromatic LED constructed by directly coating solid fluorescent CD powders on a 365 nm chip. And also a high-quality white LED was prepared by using commercial phosphor and CD powders based on the principle of three primary colors as shown in Fig. 5e and f. CD phosphor was deposited on the surface of a 365 nm UV emissive LED chip with UV curing adhesive. Through the optimization of parameters, the concentration of phosphor, CIE coordinates, color temperature, and CRI can be adjusted to control the performance of LED. As shown in Fig. 5c, a monochromatic LED based on pure CD phosphor with CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.49), CT of 5521 K, and CRI of 56.7 can be successfully fabricated. And the white LED with CIE coordinates of (0.35, 0.37), CT of 4691 K, and CRI of 91.5 (Fig. 5g) can be finally obtained. It is found that when the applied voltage was 3.0 V, the constructed white LED showed a high-quality white light, and its fluorescence spectrum is shown in Fig. 5h. The emission peak at 490 nm may be derived from the CDs phosphors, while the origin of the distinct emission peak at 605 nm can be attributed to the commercial phosphors. The fluorescence spectrum covers a range from 400 nm to 750 nm, basically covering the whole visible light region. In brief, this work opens up a new way to explore low-cost, environment-friendly, and high-performance warm white LEDs based on CD phosphors and simultaneously shows that they have broad application prospects in solid-state lighting. We anticipate that further improving the QY of CDs and the redshift of its emission will make a greater leap in white LEDs based on CD phosphors.

In summary, a simple and one-step solvothermal method for fast synthesizing solid-state fluorescent CDs without matrix was reported. The preparation method possesses the advantages of simple operation and low cost and is not involved in complex post-treatment and purification steps. The synthesized yellow-green CDs could effectively inhibit ACQ effect and show AIEE effect and bright light under UV irradiation. The yellow-green CDs exhibited an independent excitation and emission in various organic solvents. At the same time, CDs showed solvatochromism and AIE emission redshift effect. These results also demonstrate that there is the existence of complex aggregates in our CD system. In addition, besides AIEE effect in luminescence, the prepared CDs also have the characteristics of structural size uniformity, good crystal quality, and high fluorescence intensity at room temperature, and the solid-state PLQY was 25.5%. Furthermore, based on the strong fluorescence property, the CD powders were successfully applied for fabricating a monochromatic LED and a white LED with the composite of the commercial phosphors. The high-performance white LED displayed a CIE color coordinate of (0.35, 0.37), CT of 4691 K, and high CRI of 91.5. This work provides an advantageous strategy and device architecture for exploiting CDs for the applications in new optoelectronic devices. We anticipate that through future continuous research and efforts, further improvement of the performance of CDs will be achieved, so that solid-state CDs can be more widely used in lighting.

J.L. and X.G. conceived and designed the experiments. J.L. performed the experiments and analyzed the data. J.L. and X.G. wrote and revised the manuscript. X.G. supervised the whole project. All the authors revised and checked draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098), and the 111 project (No. B18038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2022.101255.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red carbon dots: optical property regulations and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhu, Y. Zhai, Z. Li, P. Zhu, S. Mao, C. Zhu, D. Du, L.A. Belfiore, J. Tang, Y. Lin, Red carbon dots: Optical property regulations and applications, Materials Today 30 (2019) 52-79.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The development of carbon dots: from the perspective of materials chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, L. Li, H. Tu, H. Zhang, D.S. Silvester, C.E. Banks, G. Zou, H. Hou, X. Ji, The development of carbon dots: From the perspective of materials chemistry, Materials Today 51 (2021) 188-207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of carbon dots with color-tunable aggregation-induced emission by nitrogen-induced intramolecular charge transfer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, L. Mo, Y. Li, X. Pan, G. Hu, B. Lei, X. Zhang, M. Zheng, J. Zhuang, Y. Liu, C. Hu, Construction of Carbon Dots with Color-Tunable Aggregation-Induced Emission by Nitrogen-Induced Intramolecular Charge Transfer, Adv Mater (2021) 2104872.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright high-colour-purity deep-blue carbon dot light-emitting diodes via efficient edge amination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yuan, Y.-K. Wang, G. Sharma, Y. Dong, X. Zheng, P. Li, A. Johnston, G. Bappi, J.Z. Fan, H. Kung, B. Chen, M.I. Saidaminov, K. Singh, O. Voznyy, O.M. Bakr, Z.-H. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Syntheses and UV/Vis-spectroscopic properties of hydrophilic 2-, 3-, and 4-pyridyl-substituted solvatochromic and halochromic pyridinium N-phenolate betaine dyes as new empirical solvent polarity indicators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Reichardt, D.Q. Che, G. Heckenkemper, G. Schafer, Syntheses and UV/Vis-spectroscopic properties of hydrophilic 2-, 3-, and 4-pyridyl-substituted solvatochromic and halochromic pyridinium N-phenolate betaine dyes as new empirical solvent polarity indicators, Eur J Org Chem 2001 (2001) 2343-2361.
Y. Hong, J.W. Lam, B.Z. Tang, Aggregation-induced emission, Chem Soc Rev 40 (2011) 5361-5388.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: an innovative luminescent nanomaterial

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shi, H. Xu, T. Yuan, T. Meng, H. Wu, J. Chang, H. Wang, X. Song, Y. Li, X. Li, Y. Zhang, W. Xie, L. Fan, Carbon dots: An innovative luminescent nanomaterial, Aggregate 3 (2021) 108.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Generating long-wavelength absorption bands with enhanced deep red fluorescence and photothermal performance in fused carbon dots aggregates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wu, J.H. Lei, B. He, C.X. Deng, Z. Tang, S. Qu, Generating long-wavelength absorption bands with enhanced deep red fluorescence and photothermal performance in fused carbon dots aggregates, Aggregate 2 (2021) 139.
